questions.explanation: Mr. Gessler was a skilled bootmaker who lived with his elder brother in London.

questions.text: What was the specialty of the boots made by the Gessler brothers?
options: They fitted perfectly and lasted very long
questions.explanation: Their boots were made only on order and represented the highest quality of craftsmanship.

questions.text: Mr. Gessler considered bootmaking to be:
options: Just a way to earn money
questions.explanation: To him, making a boot was as much an art as any other creative profession.

questions.text: Why did the Gessler brothers lose their business over time?
options: They forgot how to make boots
Competition from big firms with large advertising budgets
questions.explanation: The big firms got business by advertisement, not by work, which hurt individual craftsmen like Gessler.

questions.text: What happened to the elder Mr. Gessler?
options: He died of shock after losing a part of the shop
questions.explanation: The loss of half their shop to a bigger firm led to his decline and eventual death.

questions.text: Mr. Gessler's speech had an accent from which country?
questions.explanation: His speech had the 'flavour of German-English' as seen in his pronunciation of 'Id is an Ardt'.

questions.text: The narrator felt ________ when he had to tell Mr. Gessler that a pair of boots creaked.
questions.explanation: He felt guilty because he knew how much effort and soul Gessler put into every pair.

questions.text: How did the younger Mr. Gessler die?
questions.explanation: He put all his money into rent and leather, often skipping meals to finish his orders.

questions.text: Who took over the shop after Mr. Gessler passed away?
questions.explanation: When the narrator visited the shop for the last time, a new man told him that Gessler was gone.

options: The craftsmanship and character of Mr. Gessler
questions.explanation: The story highlights the loss of individual artistry and quality in the face of mass production.
